audit_metadata.scope: Functional bugs only — highlighting, preview, source display, hardcoded values

gaps.title: PDF line numbers are completely fake/fabricated
gaps.description: For PDFs, start_line and end_line are calculated as: `approx_start = ci * 400 + 1` and `approx_end = approx_start + len(sub.split('\n'))`. The number 400 is an arbitrary constant that has no relation to actual line positions. These fake line numbers are stored in ChromaDB metadata, then read by retrieval.py which passes them through to the frontend. The frontend uses them to build segments and display 'Lines X-Y' labels — all of which are lies.
gaps.root_cause: PDFs don't have a natural concept of 'lines'. Instead of tracking actual character offsets or omitting line data for PDFs, the code fabricates numbers using a hardcoded multiplier of 400.
gaps.impact: The 'Lines X-Y' displayed in source cards for PDF documents are nonsensical. When the React frontend's generateHighlightedHtml uses these line numbers, highlighting targets completely wrong regions of the text.
gaps.fix: For PDFs, don't store fake line numbers. Use the page number as the locator instead: `'start_line': page_idx + 1, 'end_line': page_idx + 1`. Or better, remove start_line/end_line entirely for PDFs and rely solely on the 'page' metadata field. The frontend already handles PDF sources by page (checking `source.full_content`), so the line numbers are unused for PDFs anyway.

gaps.title: Excel/CSV line numbers are also fabricated with hardcoded multiplier
gaps.description: For Excel: `start_line = 1 + ci * 10`, `end_line = (ci + 1) * 10`. For CSV: identical formula. The multiplier 10 has no relationship to actual row positions in the spreadsheet. These fake line numbers propagate through the entire pipeline to the UI.
gaps.root_cause: Same pattern as PDFs — arbitrary hardcoded multiplier instead of tracking actual positions.
gaps.impact: Source cards for Excel/CSV files show fake line ranges. If the frontend tries to highlight these ranges in the document text, it highlights wrong content.
gaps.fix: Track actual character position or row number: For Excel, use the DataFrame index range. `start_line = df.index[0] + 1` (for the portion in this chunk). Or track cumulative character offset like the TXT extractor does.

gaps.title: TXT/DOCX line tracking uses content.find() which can fail after RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
gaps.description: The code does `pos = content.find(sub, char_offset)` to locate a chunk's position in the original text. However, RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with chunk_overlap=300 means chunks overlap. When chunk N's start text also appears earlier in the document at a different position, content.find() may locate the WRONG occurrence. Also, when find() returns -1 (not found), the fallback is `start_line = 1 + ci * 10` — another hardcoded fake number.
gaps.root_cause: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter can produce chunks whose first characters match text earlier in the document. content.find() finds the first occurrence, not necessarily the correct one. The 300-char overlap makes this likely for repeated patterns.
gaps.impact: For TXT and DOCX files with repeated phrases or structures, the stored line numbers point to the wrong location. The highlighted region in the preview doesn't match the actual chunk that was retrieved.
gaps.fix: Instead of using content.find(), track positions during splitting: use splitter.split_text_with_metadata() or manually track offsets by iterating through the split results. If using find(), always search from the END of the previous chunk: `pos = content.find(sub, char_offset)`. The code already does this for char_offset, but should also handle the -1 case by incrementing from last known position: `if pos == -1: start_line = prev_end_line + 1`.

gaps.title: PDF ingestion uses pypdf but preview rendering uses pdfplumber — text mismatch breaks highlighting
gaps.description: ingest_docs.py extracts PDF text via `pypdf.PdfReader` (L31). But tools/retrieval.py L254 re-extracts with `pdfplumber.open()` for the preview. These two libraries extract text DIFFERENTLY: different whitespace, line breaks, character encoding, and handling of multi-column layouts. The chunks stored in ChromaDB (from pypdf) won't exactly match the full_content text (from pdfplumber) shown in the preview.
gaps.root_cause: Two different PDF text extraction libraries used at different stages of the same pipeline.
gaps.impact: The React frontend's generateHighlightedHtml tries to regex-match chunk text against the page text. Since the text was extracted by different libraries, exact matches fail. The highlighting either misses the correct paragraph entirely or falls back to fuzzy/word-level matching which highlights irrelevant content.
gaps.fix: Use the SAME library everywhere. Replace pypdf in ingest_docs.py with pdfplumber: `import pdfplumber; with pdfplumber.open(filepath) as pdf: for page_idx, page in enumerate(pdf.pages): text = page.extract_text() or ''`. This ensures the chunks in ChromaDB match the text shown in the preview.

gaps.title: Highlighting uses sentences from AI answer — highlights irrelevant text
gaps.description: generateHighlightedHtml extracts SENTENCES from the AI's answer (L54-56) and uses them as search terms for highlighting. Problem: the AI answer is a synthesized summary that may use different wording than the source document. For example, if the AI says 'Employees are entitled to 12 days of casual leave' but the document says 'Casual Leave: 12 days per annum', the sentence from the answer won't match. Worse, individual WORDS from the answer like 'employees', 'entitled', 'days' WILL match random unrelated paragraphs throughout the document, causing false highlights everywhere.
gaps.root_cause: Highlighting search terms are derived from the AI's paraphrased answer rather than solely from the actual retrieved chunks.
gaps.impact: Highlighted regions in the source preview don't correspond to the relevant section. Users see yellow highlights scattered across irrelevant paragraphs that happen to share common words with the AI's answer.

gaps.title: Paragraph block expansion highlights entire unrelated paragraphs
gaps.description: Lines 81-102 implement 'paragraph block logic': if ANY line in a paragraph matches even one search term, the ENTIRE paragraph block is highlighted (yellow). Since search terms include common words from the AI answer (GAP-005), a single word like 'policy' matching on one line causes every line in that paragraph to be highlighted — even if the paragraph is completely unrelated to the query.
gaps.root_cause: Over-aggressive highlighting strategy: matching one word in a paragraph highlights the whole block.
gaps.impact: Large blocks of irrelevant text are highlighted in yellow, making it impossible for the user to identify the actually relevant sentences.

gaps.title: Regex matching on whitespace-normalized text causes positional mismatches
gaps.description: Line 66 builds a regex by joining chunk words with `\\s+`: `const regexStr = escapedChunk.split(/\\s+/).filter(w => w.length > 0).join('\\s+')`. This makes the regex tolerant of whitespace differences, which is good. But L75 normalizes the line text too: `const normLine = line.replace(/\\s+/g, ' ')`. The problem: the regex can now match ACROSS what should be separate terms. For example, the chunk 'leave policy' creates regex `leave\\s+policy`, which matches 'maternity leave  policy' and 'leave  policy review' — different contexts entirely.
gaps.root_cause: Whitespace-normalized regex matching is too permissive and creates cross-boundary matches.
gaps.impact: Lines that contain the search words in a different context get incorrectly highlighted.

gaps.title: PDF source start_line/end_line set to page numbers — confused with document line numbers
gaps.description: For PDF sources (L280-281): `'start_line': pages[0], 'end_line': pages[-1]`. These are PAGE numbers being stored in fields named 'start_line' and 'end_line'. Later in the answer builder (L384), they're displayed as 'Lines: {start_line}-{end_line}'. So a PDF citing pages 3-5 shows 'Lines: 3-5' in the answer — completely misleading.
gaps.root_cause: Semantic mismatch: page numbers stored in line-number fields, then displayed as line references.
gaps.impact: Confusing display: 'Lines 1-3' actually means Pages 1-3. If a downstream component ever tries to use these for line-based highlighting, it will highlight lines 1-3 of the page text instead of the relevant section.
gaps.fix: Use separate fields for clarity. Change L384 to check for PDFs: `if 'full_content' in src: loc = f'Pages: {src["start_line"]}-{src["end_line"]}' else: loc = f'Lines: {src["start_line"]}-{src["end_line"]}'`. Or rename the fields for PDFs to 'start_page' and 'end_page'.

gaps.title: Segment merging can merge chunks from completely different sections of a document
gaps.description: The merge logic (L302-311) merges chunks whose line numbers have a gap ≤ 2. But since the ingested line numbers for some file types are fake (GAP-001, GAP-002), two chunks from completely different sections of a document might have adjacent fake line numbers and get merged into a single segment. The merged segment's content is concatenated (L325), creating a Frankenstein text block mixing unrelated content.
gaps.root_cause: Merging based on fake/inaccurate line numbers. The 'gap ≤ 2' heuristic assumes line numbers are accurate positions.
gaps.impact: Source preview shows a merged segment with content from two different parts of the document. The user sees a single highlighted block that has irrelevant text spliced into the middle of the relevant text.
gaps.fix: Don't merge by line number alone. Also check section headers: `if gap <= 2 and total_l <= 50 and g.get('section') == current_sub[-1].get('section')`. This prevents merging across different sections.

gaps.title: Source card 'content' field only shows first segment — drops all other segments
gaps.description: Line 335: `'content': file_segments[0]['content']` — only the FIRST segment's content is used for the backwards-compatible 'content' field. For text files (MD, TXT, DOCX), the React frontend falls into the `['md', 'txt', 'docx'].includes(ext)` branch (App.jsx L267-276) which uses `source.content` for generating highlighted HTML. This means only the first segment gets highlighted even when multiple segments from the same file were retrieved.
gaps.root_cause: Backwards compatibility hack that takes only the first segment's content. The React frontend path for text files uses this single 'content' field instead of iterating 'segments'.
gaps.impact: When a query matches multiple sections of the same text file, only the first section is shown in the preview. The other relevant sections are invisible — the user sees an incomplete answer context.

gaps.title: Text file preview uses source.content but ignores source.segments for pagination
gaps.description: For text files (App.jsx L267-276), togglePreview creates a 'text-snippet' previewData with `segments: source.segments || [{content: source.content}]`. However, pagination (handlePageChange L191-207) loads each segment individually. Since the first segment is shown from source.content (which only has segment 0 per GAP-010), and segments may contain the full list, paginating forward shows DIFFERENT text than what was highlighted — the highlighting was generated for segment 0 but the text changes to segment 1.
gaps.root_cause: Mismatch between the initially highlighted content (source.content = segment 0) and the paginated content (source.segments[N].content).
gaps.impact: When the user paginates through segments, the highlighting becomes wrong — it was computed for the first segment's text. Yellow marks appear at positions that correspond to words in segment 0 but are now overlaid on segment 1's different text.

gaps.title: Policy keywords are hardcoded — won't adapt to new document types
gaps.description: The policy_keywords dictionary (L41-50) is a hardcoded mapping of 8 policy types to their keywords. If new documents are uploaded with different policy types (e.g., 'anti-harassment policy', 'travel policy', 'dress code'), the retrieval pipeline won't boost them and their chunks lose the policy_match scoring bonus (0.2 in the ranking formula).
gaps.root_cause: Policy detection is manually coded instead of derived from the actual documents in the knowledge base.
gaps.impact: New policy types added by users are not boosted during retrieval. Only the 8 built-in types benefit from policy-aware ranking.
gaps.fix:
Extract policy types dynamically from ChromaDB metadata during initialization:
```python
def _load_policy_types(self):
    results = self.collection.get(include=['metadatas'])
    types = set()
    for meta in results['metadatas']:
        pt = meta.get('policy_type', '')
        if pt and pt != 'General':
            types.add(pt.lower())
    return types
```
Then in extract_policy_type, check if query words match any known type. Or remove policy boosting entirely and rely solely on the reranker (which is more general).

gaps.title: Missing comma in policy_keywords creates concatenated garbage keyword
gaps.description: Line 46: `"solicit" "external work"` — Python silently concatenates adjacent string literals, producing the single string `"solicitexternal work"`. Neither 'solicit' nor 'external work' individually will match any query for moonlighting detection.
gaps.root_cause: Typo: missing comma between two string literals in a list.
gaps.impact: Queries about 'soliciting clients' or 'external work' won't trigger the moonlighting policy boost. Retrieval falls back to general ranking.

gaps.title: Markdown policy header regex only matches one specific format
gaps.description: Line 190: `re.match(r'^\s*\*\*\d+\)\s+.*Policy\*\*', line)` — this only matches policy headers in the exact format `**8) Whistle Blower Policy**` (bold, numbered, ending with 'Policy'). It won't detect: `## Leave Policy`, `### Anti-Harassment Policy`, `**Travel Reimbursement Guidelines**`, or any other header format. Only documents using this exact convention get policy_type metadata.
gaps.root_cause: Hardcoded regex pattern that only matches one document's formatting convention.
gaps.impact: Markdown documents with different header formatting don't get policy_type metadata attached to their chunks. The policy boosting in retrieval.py (filter_by_policy_type) doesn't work for these documents, degrading retrieval quality.

gaps.title: Default start_line=1 and end_line=5 when metadata is missing — wrong display
gaps.description: Lines 174-175: `'start_line': int(metadata.get('start_line') or 1)` and `'end_line': int(metadata.get('end_line') or 5)`. When metadata is missing (e.g., from a document type with no line tracking), all chunks default to 'Lines 1-5'. Multiple chunks from the same file all show the same range. The segment merger (L302-311) then merges them all because the gap between identical ranges is 0.
gaps.root_cause: Hardcoded default values used instead of None/null to indicate 'line position unknown'.
gaps.impact: All chunks without explicit line metadata appear to be from the same location (Lines 1-5). They get merged into one giant segment. The preview shows 'Lines 1-5' but the content may span hundreds of lines.
gaps.fix: Use None for unknown positions and handle it downstream: `'start_line': int(metadata.get('start_line')) if metadata.get('start_line') is not None else None`. In the merger, skip merging when either position is None.

gaps.title: Text file preview passes source.content but source.chunks is often empty
gaps.description: togglePreview for text files (L268) calls: `generateHighlightedHtml(source.content || '', source.chunks || [], ...)`. But for text-type sources, the 'chunks' field is never set by retrieval.py (only PDF sources get 'chunks' on L279). So source.chunks is always undefined, defaulting to []. With no chunks, the highlighting depends entirely on the AI answer text and query words — which per GAP-005 highlights wrong content.
gaps.root_cause: retrieval.py only attaches 'chunks' (matched text snippets) to PDF sources, not to text/MD/DOCX sources.
gaps.impact: Text file previews have no chunk-based highlighting. Falls back to answer-derived search terms which highlight irrelevant text.
gaps.fix:
In retrieval.py, attach chunks to text sources too:
```python
final_other_chunks.append({
    ...
    'chunks': [seg['content'] for seg in file_segments],  # ADD THIS
})
```

gaps.title: Quick questions are hardcoded in both frontends
gaps.description: Streamlit (app.py L54-60) has 5 hardcoded quick questions. React (App.jsx L6-15) has 8 different hardcoded quick questions. These can't be customized or updated without editing source code. They also don't match — the two frontends show different questions.
gaps.root_cause: Quick questions are inline constants in both frontend files instead of being served from the backend or a config file.
gaps.impact: An admin cannot customize the quick questions without deploying code changes. The two frontends present different quick actions, confusing users who switch between them.
gaps.fix: Serve quick questions from the API: Add `@app.get('/api/quick-questions') def quick_questions(): return {'questions': [load from config or DB]}`. Both frontends fetch from this endpoint on mount.

gaps.title: Greeting words are hardcoded — won't match non-English greetings
gaps.description: app.py L183: `greeting_words = {'hi', 'hello', 'hey', 'hola', ...}` — this is a manually maintained set. Adding new greetings requires code changes. 'Hola' is included but not other languages. Also inconsistent: the React frontend has no greeting detection at all, so the same greeting gets a canned response in Streamlit but goes to the full agent in React.
gaps.root_cause: Greeting detection is a hardcoded set in only one frontend.
gaps.impact: Inconsistent behavior between frontends. Minor: some greetings bypass the agent while others don't.
gaps.fix: Remove greeting detection entirely and let the agent handle it (the system prompt already instructs it to decline non-HR queries). Or move it to the backend so both frontends behave consistently.
